current_value: A prefix is a part that goes in FRONT of a base word. It changes the meaning, but it never changes the base word's spelling. Prefix + base — two chunks, snap them together. | Prefix spelling is fixed and base spelling is preserved: re + play = replay, un + tie = untie. Spell each chunk, then join them with no letters lost. | Two chunks: spell the prefix, spell the base, push them together. Nothing falls off.

current_value: A suffix is a part that goes on the END of a base word. It changes the word's job (slow → slowly) but the base usually keeps its spelling. Find the base, spell it, add the ending. | Suffix spelling is fixed and, by default, base spelling is preserved: slow + ly = slowly, kind + ness = kindness. Spell base then suffix as two chunks. | Say the word in two chunks — base, then ending — and spell each chunk.

current_value: Long words are built from a smaller word plus extra parts. Find the small word first — it's the Keeper of the meaning. If you can spell the base, most of the long word is already done. | A base word is a complete word that can stand alone (help in helpful, play in replaying). Spot the base first, spell it, then add the extra parts around it. | Before you spell a long word, ask: what small word is hiding inside?

current_value: When you add a part to a word, the base word usually keeps every one of its letters. The Keeper doesn't drop letters just because a new part arrives. | Base spelling is usually preserved when affixes are added: hope + less = hopeless, joy + ful = joyful. (Some suffix rules change the ending — that's a different skill — but the default is: keep the base whole.) | Spell the base first, then bolt the part on — don't lose any letters at the join, even if it makes a double letter.

current_value: Some words are two whole words glued into one: foot + ball = football, rain + bow = rainbow. Both little words keep ALL their letters — two Keepers, one word. | Closed compounds join two complete base words with no letters lost, even where letters double up at the join: book + keeper = bookkeeper. | Find BOTH little words first. Spell each one whole, then push them together — nothing falls off at the join.

current_value: Some two-word teams stay apart (ice cream), and some hold hands with a hyphen (well-known). The words themselves stay perfectly spelled — the skill is knowing whether they're glued, hyphenated, or separate. | Open compounds keep a space (ice cream, living room); hyphenated compounds join with - (twenty-one, part-time). Each word part keeps its full spelling. | Spell each word perfectly first; then ask — glued, hyphen, or space? Learn each team's style like a name.

current_value: This 'not' prefix is a shape-shifter: it copies the start of the base so it's easier to say. Before most letters it's in- (invisible). Before m or p it's im- (impossible). Before l it's il- (illegal). Before r it's ir- (irregular). | The negative prefix assimilates to the base's first letter: im- before m/p, il- before l, ir- before r, in- elsewhere. This often creates a double letter: il + legal = illegal, ir + regular = irregular. | Look at the FIRST letter of the base — the prefix copies it: l → il (illegal, ll), r → ir (irregular, rr), m/p → im.

current_value: un- is a tiny part that means NOT or the OPPOSITE. Stick it on the front and the word flips: kind → unkind, lock → unlock. The base word keeps every letter. | un- is always spelled u-n, attached before a base word whose spelling stays the same: un + happy = unhappy, un + necessary = unnecessary (double n stays). | un- is always just u-n, and the base keeps all its letters — even if that makes nn.

current_value: The ancient Greeks gave us word-treasures too: tele = far (telephone, telescope), phon = sound, graph = write/draw, photo = light. Greek roots carry secret signs — ph says /f/, ch says /k/ — that mark a word as Greek. | Greek roots keep stable spelling and distinctive graphemes: phon (sound), graph (write), photo (light), tele (far), scope (look), micro (small). ph=/f/ and ch=/k/ signal Greek origin. | An /f/ in a science-y word is often the Greek sign ph — telephone, graph, photo. Spot the Greek treasure marks.

current_value: Two thousand years ago the Romans spoke Latin — and their word-bricks are still inside our words! port = carry (transport, export, import). struct = build (structure, construct). Spot the Roman brick and half the word is spelled. | Latin roots keep stable spelling across word families: port (carry), struct (build), rupt (break), tract (pull), dict (say), scrib/script (write). Spell root as a fixed chunk, then add affixes. | Find the Roman brick first — port, struct, rupt — spell it as one chunk, then add the front and end parts.

current_value: A root is the family surname — it's spelled the same on every family member. act: act, actor, action, active, react. The endings change, the sounds shift, but a-c-t never breaks. | Root spelling is invariant across derivatives: act/action/actor, medic/medical/medicine, sign/signal/signature. Anchor the root, then vary the affixes. | Learn the surname once — act, medic, sign — and trust it in every relative, whatever the ending sounds like.

current_value: School subject words look scary until you see the bricks: thermometer = therm (heat) + meter (measure). Triangle = tri (three) + angle. Spell root-by-root. | Break the scary word into bricks you know: therm + o + meter. Spell each brick — the word spells itself.

current_value: -ful means FULL OF (hopeful = full of hope). -less means WITHOUT (hopeless = without hope). They're opposite twins that snap onto the same bases. Big trap: -ful has ONE l, even though it means 'full'. | -ful is always spelled with a single l (careful, not carefull); -less always has double s. Base is preserved: care + ful, care + less. | The suffix -ful lost an l on its journey: full of care = careful, ONE l. And -less always keeps its ss.
